510	\begin{lemma}
511	
512	\leanok
513	[Global-sections constancy: two $\bar k$-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
514	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
516	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
517	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
518	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
519	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
520	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and let $W$ be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
521	  morphism $w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k$ that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
522	  type. Let $g \colon W \to V$ be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme $V$. Then any two
523	  $\bar k$-points of $W$ --- i.e.\ any two sections $a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W$ of $w$ (so
524	  $a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1$ and $b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1$) --- satisfy $a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g$.
525	
526	  \medskip
527	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
528	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ collapses the finite extension $\Gamma(W)/\bar k$; integrality of
529	  $W$ makes $\Gamma(W)$ a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
530	  $\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}$ is what upgrades $\Gamma(W)$ to a \emph{field} (without it
531	  $\mathbb A^1$, with $\Gamma = \bar k[t]$ and two distinct $\bar k$-points, is a counterexample);
532	  $\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}$ gives finiteness of $\Gamma(W)$ over $\bar k$; and
533	  $[\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]$ is what lets $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$ pin the morphism by its ring map
534	  on global sections.
535	\end{lemma}
536	
537	\begin{proof}
538	
539	\leanok
540	  Because $W$ is integral and $w$ is universally closed, the global sections
541	  $\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)$ form a field ($\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}$), and the structure
542	  ring map $w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)$ (the map $\mathtt{wk.appTop}$ after the canonical
543	  identification $\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k$) is integral / module-finite
544	  ($\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}$). Since $\bar k$ is algebraically closed and
545	  $\Gamma(W)$ is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
546	  ($\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}$); thus $\Gamma(W) = \bar k$ and
547	  $w^\sharp$ is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
548	  $a$, $b$ left-invert $w^\sharp$ on global sections (they are sections of $w$), so they induce the
549	  \emph{same} ring map $a^\sharp = b^\sharp$ on $\Gamma(W)$ (cancel the epimorphism $w^\sharp$). A
550	  morphism into the \emph{affine} $V$ is pinned by its global-sections ring map
551	  ($\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}$), whence $a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g$. This realises, without
552	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
553	  quoted from Mumford above.
554	\end{proof}

561	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract}
562	  % NOTE: (iter-162 review) project-bespoke (elementary topology + algebra); NO external source.
563	  % LANDED axiom-clean as `AlgebraicGeometry.isIntegral_of_retract`, stated generically for any
564	  % retract `r : S ⟶ T`, `pr : T ⟶ S` with `r ≫ pr = 𝟙 S` of an integral `T` (more general than
565	  % the `X`/`X×Y`/`p_1` instance below). PROOF DIVERGENCE (cosmetic, sound either way): the Lean
566	  % proves reducedness PER-STALK (each `S.stalk x` embeds via `pr.stalkMap` into the reduced
567	  % `T.stalk`, then `isReduced_of_isReduced_stalk`), not via the global-sections split-injection
568	  % the prose below describes. Plan/blueprint-writer may align the prose to the stalk route.
569	  Let $X \times Y$ be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
570	  $\bar k$). Suppose $X$ is a \emph{retract} of $X \times Y$: there is a section
571	  $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ of the first projection $p_1$, i.e.\ $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X$.
572	  Then $X$ is integral.
573	\end{lemma}
574	
575	\begin{proof}
576	
577	\leanok
578	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
579	
580	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X$ makes the pullback on global
581	  sections $p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)$ split
582	  injective (it is left-inverted by $s^\sharp$). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
583	  $\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)$ embeds $\mathcal O_X(X)$ into a reduced ring, so it too is
584	  reduced ($\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}$ on the split injection); hence $X$ is reduced.
585	
586	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection $p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X$ is surjective because it
587	  admits the section $s$ (every point of $X$ is $p_1(s(\cdot))$). The continuous image of an
588	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as $X \times Y$ is irreducible and $p_1$ is a
589	  continuous surjection onto $X$, the space of $X$ is irreducible.
590	
591	  Reduced and irreducible together give that $X$ is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
592	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
593	  $X_y \cong X$ must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
594	\end{proof}

644	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
645	
646	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-$0$ base case. The two
647	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
648	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
649	G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ on $\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
650	the base case ``every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
651	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one axis of the
652	decomposition, giving $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense $\mathbb G_m$. The
653	classical $\mathbb G_a$-additive homomorphism step
654	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
655	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
656	\emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
657	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
658	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb
659	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
660	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
661	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
662	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-$0$
663	path. The genus-$0$ output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
664	(here: regular) map $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant''.
665	
666	\begin{remark}
667	  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
668	  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-$0$ path.} An earlier draft of
669	  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
670	  base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ constant''. That was a misreading. The base case
671	  (Proposition~3.9, the $d = 1$ case of Proposition~3.10) follows directly from the Rigidity
672	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
673	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
674	  action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point $0$ collapses one
675	  axis of the decomposition, yielding $f(\lambda x) = f(x)$ and hence constancy on the dense
676	  $\mathbb G_m$ (the primary $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; see
677	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
678	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)$ (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
679	  Milne-faithful $\mathbb G_a$-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
680	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ is already a
681	  total morphism out of the complete $\mathbb P^1$, no defect map needs extending over a complete
682	  surface (this corrects the earlier claim that the surface extension was on the genus-$0$ path;
683	  see \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) enters only for
684	  projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties, polarizations, and the
685	  Poincar\'e sheaf --- none on the genus-$0$ path. For Route~A: the Albanese universal property
686	  that the positive-genus arm needs (Milne Proposition~6.1/6.4, \S III.6) is \emph{also} cube-free,
687	  resting on Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) + Corollary~1.2/1.5 --- which is
688	  precisely why Theorem~3.2 and Corollary~1.2 are retained in this chapter as Route-A inputs.
689	\end{remark}

725	  \emph{Lean encoding.} In the project $A$ is a group object \texttt{[GrpObj A]} (not assumed
726	  commutative). The ``$+$'' here is the binary operation that \texttt{GrpObj} induces on each
727	  hom-set $\mathrm{Hom}(X, A)$: for $u, v \colon X \to A$, $u + v$ is the composite
728	  $X \xrightarrow{\langle u, v\rangle} A \times A \xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{mul}\;} A$ (and
729	  $f \circ p + g \circ q$ is built this way from the projections). The decomposition is therefore
730	  read \emph{multiplicatively} as $h = (f \circ p) \cdot (g \circ q)$ in the group
731	  $\mathrm{Hom}(V \times W, A)$, and does \emph{not} require $A$ commutative; commutativity of
732	  abelian varieties (Milne Cor 1.4, a separate consequence of \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom})
733	  is needed only to rewrite the product symmetrically/additively, not for the statement or its
734	  proof. The prover should infer the hom-set group operation from the \texttt{GrpObj} instance,
735	  not from a \texttt{CommGrpObj} instance.
736	\end{lemma}
737	
738	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Set f=h|V×{w0}, g=h|{v0}×W, and identify V×{w0} and {v0}×W with
739	% V and W. On points, f(v) = h(v,w0) and g(w) = h(v0,w), and so φ =df h − (f∘p + g∘q) is the
740	% map that sends (v,w) ↦ h(v,w) − h(v,w0) − h(v0,w). Thus φ(V×{w0}) = 0 = φ({v0}×W) and so the
741	% theorem shows that φ = 0." [Milne, Cor 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9--10.]
742	\begin{proof}
743	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma}
744	  \leanok
745	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
746	  Define $f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}$ and $g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}$, identifying
747	  $V \times \{w_0\} \cong V$ and $\{v_0\} \times W \cong W$; on points $f(v) = h(v, w_0)$ and
748	  $g(w) = h(v_0, w)$. Form the difference
749	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
750	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
751	  (the subtraction is in the group $A$, i.e.\ $\varphi$ is the composite of
752	  $(h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))$ with the group law of $A$). Then $\varphi$ vanishes on both
753	  axes: $\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)$ (direct computation, using
754	  $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$). In particular $\varphi$ collapses the complete axis $V \times \{w_0\}$ to
755	  the single point $0 \in A$, so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with $V$
756	  complete and $V \times W$ geometrically irreducible) $\varphi$ factors through the projection
757	  $q$; but it also vanishes on $\{v_0\} \times W$, which is a section of $q$, so the factoring
758	  morphism is identically $0$, i.e.\ $\varphi \equiv 0$. Hence $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$.
759	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers $f$ and $g$ as the
760	  axis-restrictions of $h$.
761	\end{proof}

763	\begin{lemma}
764	
765	\leanok
766	[A pointed regular map of abelian varieties is a homomorphism (Milne Corollary 1.2)]
767	  \label{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}
768	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.av_regularMap_isHom_of_zero}
769	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
770	  % NOTE: (iter-163 review) The Lean formalizes the POINTED direction
771	  % (η[A] ≫ α = η[B] ⟹ IsMonHom α), not the full "composite of a homomorphism with a
772	  % translation" form — which is what the genus-0 consumer needs. The conclusion IsMonHom α
773	  % (one_hom + mul_hom) is a faithful, slightly stronger rendering of "α is a homomorphism".
774	  % As with lem:hom_additivity_over_product, the signature carries the product-level instances
775	  % [GeometricallyIrreducible (A⊗A).hom], [LocallyOfFiniteType (A⊗A).hom], [IsReduced (A⊗A).left]
776	  % ("A×A is a variety"), free for a genuine abelian variety A but carried explicitly because
777	  % Mathlib lacks the product-instance inference. (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter163.)
778	  % NOTE: (iter-164 review) iter-164 hygiene dropped the B-side instances [Smooth B.hom] and
779	  % [GeometricallyIrreducible B.hom] from the Lean signature; the target B now requires only
780	  % [GrpObj B] [IsProper B.hom]. (The source-side A retains the full abelian-variety instance
781	  % set.) The Lean signature is strictly more general on B than the prose ("α : A → B of
782	  % abelian varieties") demands. The lean-auditor flagged the symmetric A-side
783	  % [Smooth A.hom]/[GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom] as ALSO unused — kept this iter as a
784	  % minimal-change follow-up. (lean-vs-blueprint-checker avr-iter164.)
785	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 15)
786	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.2. Every regular map α:A→B of abelian varieties is the composite
787	  % of a homomorphism with a translation."
788	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
789	  Every regular map $\alpha \colon A \to B$ of abelian varieties over $\bar k$ is the composite
790	  of a homomorphism with a translation; equivalently, if $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$ then $\alpha$ is a
791	  homomorphism, i.e.\ $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$ for all $a, a' \in A$.
792	\end{lemma}
793	
794	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The regular map α will send the k-rational point 0 of A to a
795	% k-rational point b of B. After composing α with translation by −b, we may assume that
796	% α(0)=0. Consider the map φ:A×A→B, φ(a,a′)=α(a+a′) − α(a) − α(a′). By this we mean that φ is
797	% the difference of the two regular maps A×A →(m)→ A →(α)→ B and A×A →(α×α)→ B×B →(m)→ B, which
798	% is a regular map. Then φ(A×0)=0=φ(0×A) and so φ=0. This means that α is a homomorphism."
799	% [Milne, Cor 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9.]
800	\begin{proof}
801	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
802	  \leanok
803	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
804	  The image $\alpha(0_A)$ is a $\bar k$-point $b$ of $B$; composing $\alpha$ with translation by
805	  $-b$ we may assume $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$, and it suffices to show such an $\alpha$ is a
806	  homomorphism. Form
807	  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]
808	  the difference of the two regular maps $A \times A \xrightarrow{m} A \xrightarrow{\alpha} B$
809	  and $A \times A \xrightarrow{\alpha \times \alpha} B \times B \xrightarrow{m} B$. Then
810	  $\varphi(0_A, 0_A) = 0$ and $\varphi$ vanishes on both axes,
811	  $\varphi(A \times 0) = 0 = \varphi(0 \times A)$ (since $\alpha(0) = 0$). By the additive
812	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (with $V = W = A$), the axis-restrictions
813	  of $\varphi$ being $0$ force $\varphi \equiv 0$; equivalently, $\varphi$ collapses the complete
814	  axis $A \times 0$ and vanishes on $0 \times A$, so Rigidity gives $\varphi = 0$. Hence
815	  $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$, i.e.\ $\alpha$ is a homomorphism.
816	\end{proof}

822	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 3.2 (with Theorem 3.1 and Lemma 3.3), \S I.3, doc p.~16--18 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 22--24)

826	  % normal variety V to a complete variety W is defined on an open subset U of V whose
827	  % complement V∖U has codimension ≥ 2."
828	  % SOURCE QUOTE (Lemma 3.3, used in the proof): "LEMMA 3.3. Let φ:V ⇢ G be a rational map from
829	  % a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either φ is defined on all of V or the points
830	  % where it is not defined form a closed subset of pure codimension 1 in V (i.e., a finite
831	  % union of prime divisors)."

834	  \textbf{Route-A-only (iter-164).} This extension lemma is \emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical
835	  path; the 4th route (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}, \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}) closes the
836	  base case without it. It is retained here only as an input to Route~A (the positive-genus
837	  Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6) and carries the route's riskiest sub-build
838	  (\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}).
839	
840	  A rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$ from a nonsingular variety $V$ over
841	  $\bar k$ to an abelian variety $A$ is defined on the whole of $V$.
842	\end{lemma}
843	
844	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma." [Milne, Thm 3.2, \S I.3,
845	% doc p.~17.]
846	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Thm 3.1, general case): "Let U be the largest subset on which φ is
847	% defined, and suppose that V∖U has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor Z in V∖U.
848	% Because V is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete valuation ring O_Z with field of
849	% fractions k(V). The map φ defines a morphism of schemes Spec(k(V)) → W, which the valuative
850	% criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a morphism Spec(O_Z) → W.
851	% This implies that φ has a representative defined on an open subset that meets Z in a nonempty
852	% set, which is a contradiction." [Milne, Thm 3.1, \S I.3, doc p.~17.]
853	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Lemma 3.3, the difference-map): "Define a rational map Φ:V×V ⇢ G,
854	% (x,y) ↦ φ(x)·φ(y)⁻¹. ... Thus φ is defined at x if and only if Φ is defined at (x,x). ...
855	% Suppose φ is not defined at x. Then for some f ∈ Im(Φ*), (x,x) ∈ div(f)₁, and clearly Φ is
856	% not defined at the points (y,y) ∈ Δ ∩ div(f)₁. This is a subset of pure codimension one in Δ,
857	% and when we identify it with a subset of V, it is a subset of V of codimension one passing
858	% through x on which φ is not defined." [Milne, Lemma 3.3, \S I.3, doc p.~17--18.]
859	\begin{proof}
860	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2.}
861	  The statement is Theorem~3.1 combined with Lemma~3.3. By Theorem~3.1 (a consequence of the
862	  valuative criterion of properness, Mathlib \texttt{IsProper}/\texttt{ValuativeCriterion},
863	  applied at the discrete valuation ring of each codimension-$1$ point), a rational map from the
864	  normal $V$ to the complete $A$ is defined outside a closed set of codimension $\geq 2$. By
865	  Lemma~3.3, the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a \emph{group} variety is either
866	  empty or pure of codimension $1$: forming the difference map
867	  $\Phi(x, y) = \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow A$, one shows
868	  $\varphi$ is defined at $x$ iff $\Phi$ is defined at the diagonal point $(x, x)$, and an
869	  indeterminacy point produces a pure-codimension-$1$ component of the non-domain. The two
870	  facts are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty; hence $\alpha$ is everywhere
871	  defined.
872	\end{proof}

874	\begin{remark}
875	  \label{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}
876	  \textbf{Formalisation risk: the pure-codimension-$1$ step (Lemma 3.3) has no Mathlib
877	  Weil-divisor support.} Theorem~3.1's everywhere-extension-outside-codimension-$2$ is within
878	  reach of Mathlib's valuative criterion of properness
879	  (\texttt{ValuativeCriterion}/\texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}), applied at the
880	  discrete valuation ring $\mathcal O_{V, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$. But Lemma~3.3,
881	  which rules out codimension-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety, is proved in
882	  Milne via Weil-divisor theory ($\mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_1$, prime divisors,
883	  tangent cones) for which Mathlib currently has \emph{no} usable API at this generality. This
884	  is the route's riskiest sub-build. An open question for the prover: whether a \emph{pointwise}
885	  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ separately, that the
886	  morphism $\Spec k(V) \to A$ extends over $\Spec \mathcal O_{V, Z}$ using only that $A$ is
887	  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at $Z$ without
888	  globally building $\mathrm{div}(f)$) --- side-steps building divisor theory entirely.
889	
890	  \textbf{This surface extension is \emph{not} on the genus-$0$ critical path (corrected, iter-164).}
891	  An earlier draft routed the genus-$0$ base case through this extension, by collapsing the
892	  additive-defect map $\psi(x, y) = f(x + y) - f(x) - f(y)$ on the \emph{affine} surface
893	  $\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a$ and then extending $\psi$ over the complete surface
894	  $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ --- which genuinely \emph{would} need Theorem~3.2's surface case
895	  (Milne Theorem~3.4 / Lemma~3.3). That detour is unnecessary. The 4th route
896	  (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}) replaces the two-variable affine defect map by the
897	  \emph{total} translation action $\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1$
898	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): $h := \sigma \fatsemi f$ is a total morphism on $\mathbb P^1
899	  \times \mathbb G_a$ (no extension needed), and the complete-source factor is $\mathbb P^1$
900	  itself, so the additive decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} applies directly.
901	  Consequently \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Theorem~3.2), the codim-$1$ indeterminacy
902	  sub-build (Lemma~3.3), and the Weil-divisor gap discussed above are all confined to
903	  \textbf{Route~A} (the positive-genus Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6); they are
904	  retained in this chapter solely as Route-A inputs and may be deferred until Route~A consumes
905	  them. The genus-$0$ close no longer depends on any of them.
906	\end{remark}

908	\section{The genus-$0$ base objects and the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action (the primary route)}
909	
910	\begin{definition}
911	[Concrete genus-$0$ base objects: $\mathbb P^1$, $\mathbb G_a$, $\mathbb G_m$ over $\bar k$]
912	  \label{def:genus0_base_objects}
913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar}
914	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of standard objects; the mathematics is textbook (Hartshorne
915	  % I.2--3 for the projective line; the group laws on $\mathbb A^1$ are elementary). No external
916	  % % SOURCE QUOTE is needed for the Lean encoding itself.
917	  Work over $\Spec \bar k$ with $\bar k$ algebraically closed. The primary $\mathbb G_m$-scaling
918	  route (and its $\mathbb G_a$-additive alternative) needs only three concrete objects of
919	  $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$:
920	  \begin{itemize}
921	    \item $\mathbb P^1 = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, the projective line --- a smooth proper
922	      geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$ (Mathlib: projective space, or $\mathrm{Proj}$
923	      of the graded polynomial ring; \lean name \texttt{ProjectiveLineBar}). It carries
924	      the distinguished $\bar k$-points $0$, $1$, $\infty$ and the two standard affine charts
925	      $\mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$ (coordinate $x$) and
926	      $\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0\}$ (coordinate $u = 1/x$).
927	    \item $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k}$, the affine line as the \emph{additive} group object
928	      (Mathlib: \texttt{AffineSpace}\,$\mathbb A(1; \Spec \bar k)$ with the $\mathtt{GrpObj}$
929	      additive structure $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$, identity $0$; \lean name \texttt{Ga}).
930	      As a scheme $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$, and the open
931	      immersion $\mathbb G_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb P^1$ has dense (cofinite) image.
932	    \item $\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\} = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0, \infty\}$, the
933	      \emph{multiplicative} group object (group law $(x, y) \mapsto xy$, identity $1$; \lean name
934	      \texttt{Gm}). Its open immersion into $\mathbb P^1$ also has dense image.
935	  \end{itemize}
936	  All three are of finite type over $\bar k$; $\mathbb P^1$ is proper, while $\mathbb G_a$ and
937	  $\mathbb G_m$ are affine (hence not complete). These are the only ``genus-$0$'' geometric inputs
938	  the base case needs.
939	\end{definition}

1035	\subsection*{Chart cover and chart-ring iso (formalisation infrastructure)}
1036	
1037	The next three declarations are iter-168 formalisation infrastructure that the chartwise
1038	construction of the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
1039	and its downstream consumers (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}) ultimately rest on.
1040	They realise, at the Lean level, the standard ``affine chart of $\mathbb P^1$ is $\mathbb
1041	A^1$'' picture: a two-chart affine open cover of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ by $D_+(X_0)$ and
1042	$D_+(X_1)$; the identification of each chart ring with the one-variable polynomial ring
1043	$\bar k[u]$; and the resulting reducedness of $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ as a scheme. None
1044	of them is on the genus-$0$ critical path conceptually, but each is consumed by the Lean
1045	formalisation downstream.

1070	\begin{definition}
1071	[Chart-ring iso: $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \cong \bar k[u]$]
1072	  \label{def:proj_chart_ring_iso}

1079	  degree-zero part $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ of the localization of the standard
1080	  $\mathbb N$-graded polynomial ring $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ at $X_i$ --- is isomorphic as a
1081	  $\bar k$-algebra to the one-variable polynomial ring $\bar k[u]$, where the affine
1082	  chart coordinate is $u = X_{1-i}/X_i$. This is the formalisation-level realisation of
1083	  the standard ``affine chart of $\mathbb P^1$ is $\mathbb A^1$'' identification expressed
1084	  at the homogeneous-localization level. The forward direction $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal
1085	  A\,X_i \to \bar k[u]$ is built via $\mathrm{Localization.awayLift}$ from the chart
1086	  evaluation $X_i \mapsto 1$, $X_{1-i} \mapsto u$; the inverse direction is built via the
1087	  universal property of $\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k$, sending $u$ to the
1088	  homogeneous-localization element $X_{1-i}/X_i \in \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$; the
1089	  two directions are packaged as a $\mathrm{RingEquiv}$ via $\mathrm{RingEquiv.ofRingHom}$
1090	  applied to the two round-trip identities.

1120	\begin{lemma}
1121	[$\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ is reduced]
1122	  \label{lem:projlinebar_isReduced}
1123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBar_isReduced}
1124	  \uses{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
1125	  % Archon-original Lean realisation of an elementary textbook fact (projective space over
1126	  % a field is reduced) via the explicit chart cover of \cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
1127	  % and the canonical $\mathrm{val}$-injection of a homogeneous localization into the
1128	  % corresponding plain localization. No external % SOURCE QUOTE needed.
1129	  The scheme underlying $\overline{\mathbb P^1}$ is reduced. By the two-chart affine cover
1130	  (\cref{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}) it suffices to show that each chart ring is
1131	  reduced. The chart-$i$ ring $\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$ embeds into the ordinary
1132	  localization $\bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]$ of the polynomial ring $\bar k[X_0, X_1]$ at
1133	  the powers of $X_i$ via the canonical injection $\mathrm{val} \colon
1134	  \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \hookrightarrow \bar k[X_0, X_1][X_i^{-1}]$
1135	  ($\mathtt{HomogeneousLocalization.val\_injective}$); the codomain is a localization of a
1136	  polynomial ring at a non-zero-divisor (the powers of $X_i$), hence a domain, hence
1137	  reduced. A subring of a reduced ring is reduced
1138	  ($\mathtt{Function.Injective.isDomain}$ on the $\mathrm{val}$-embedding), so each chart
1139	  ring is a domain --- in particular reduced --- and the global reducedness follows.
1140	\end{lemma}

1142	\begin{definition}
1143	[The $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1$ (primary), and the $\mathbb G_a$-translation companion $\sigma$]
1144	  \label{def:gaTranslationP1}
1145	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1}
1146	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects}
1147	  % NOTE (iter-170 decision): the iter-169 escalation surface CLOSED — the
1148	  % planner committed to option (c) inline chart-glue at scale (NOT option (a)
1149	  % Mathlib upstream PR, NOT option (b) accept `[CharZero]` fallback). The
1150	  % commitment is a bounded 3-4-iter decomposition of the chart-glue, with
1151	  % named per-iter milestones in `iter/iter-170/plan.md`. The Lean body of
1152	  % `gmScalingP1` still ships as a typed `sorry` THIS iter; iter-170 attacks
1153	  % Step A (chart-side ring maps `gmScalingP1_chart_i_ringMap`) + the
1154	  % `homogeneousLocalizationAwayIso_aux_left` residual (the iso's lone sorry,
1155	  % needed for Step B). iter-171/iter-172/iter-173 carry the rest.
1156	  % See `analogies/gmscaling-deep.md` for the full 6-step decomposition.
1157	  % Archon-original Lean encoding; the chart computations below are elementary and characteristic-free.
1158	  % No external % SOURCE QUOTE for the encoding. The mathematical fact -- $\mathbb G_m$ acts on
1159	  % $\mathbb P^1$ by scalings fixing $0$ and $\infty$, $\mathbb G_a$ by translations fixing $\infty$
1160	  % -- is standard (the Borel subgroup of $\mathrm{PGL}_2$ acting on $\mathbb P^1$).
1161	  % NOTE (iter-164/167): the load-bearing action for the PRIMARY genus-0 route is now the
1162	  % MULTIPLICATIVE scaling action $\sigma_\times$ (\lean name gmScalingP1, landed iter-165/166;
1163	  % pinned in this block's \lean{...} hook). The $\mathbb G_a$-translation $\sigma$ (\lean name
1164	  % gaTranslationP1 [expected]) is retained only for the demoted additive alternative and is
1165	  % intentionally not yet a Lean target.
1166	  \textbf{The scaling action (primary).} The \emph{multiplicative scaling action}
1167	  \[ \sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1168	     \text{``}\sigma_\times(x, \lambda) = \lambda x\text{''} \]
1169	  (\lean name \texttt{gmScalingP1}) is a \emph{genuine total} scheme morphism on all of
1170	  $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ --- no indeterminacy, characteristic-free (M\"obius scaling
1171	  fixing both $0$ and $\infty$) --- defined chartwise:
1172	  \begin{itemize}
1173	    \item On $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ (target chart $x$): $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$,
1174	      a polynomial map, regular everywhere.
1175	    \item Near $\infty$ (target chart $u = 1/x$): the target coordinate $1/(\lambda x)$ rewrites as
1176	      $u/\lambda$, regular everywhere on this chart because $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$ is invertible.
1177	  \end{itemize}
1178	  The two target charts cover the image and overlap consistently, so $\sigma_\times$ is defined on
1179	  the whole surface. It is a left action of the group $\mathbb G_m$ on $\mathbb P^1$:
1180	  $\sigma_\times(x, 1) = x$ and $\sigma_\times(\sigma_\times(x, \lambda), \mu) = \sigma_\times(x,
1181	  \lambda\mu)$. Crucially it has \emph{two fixed points}, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ and
1182	  $\sigma_\times(\infty, \lambda) = \infty$ for all $\lambda$; the fixed point $0$ is what makes the
1183	  $W$-axis of the additive decomposition collapse in the primary base-case proof
1184	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}).
1185	
1186	  \medskip
1187	  \noindent\textbf{Companion: the $\mathbb G_a$-translation action (demoted route only).} For the
1188	  Milne-faithful additive alternative we also record the \emph{translation action}
1189	  \[ \sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1, \qquad
1190	     \text{``}\sigma(x, y) = x + y\text{''} \]
1191	  (\lean name \texttt{gaTranslationP1} [expected]), likewise a \emph{total} scheme morphism on all
1192	  of $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a$, defined chartwise: on $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_a$
1193	  (chart $x$) it is the polynomial map $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$; near $\infty$ ($u = 1/x$) the
1194	  coordinate $1/(x + y)$ rewrites as $u/(1 + u y)$, regular at $u = 0$ (value $0$ there --- $\sigma$
1195	  fixes $\infty$ for every $y$), while the locus $x + y = 0$ is covered by the chart $x$ on which it
1196	  is the finite value $0$. It is a left action of $\mathbb G_a$ on $\mathbb P^1$ fixing $\infty$:
1197	  $\sigma(x, 0) = x$ and $\sigma(\sigma(x, y), y') = \sigma(x, y + y')$. This companion underlies
1198	  only the alternative proof of \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} and the demoted $\mathbb
1199	  G_a$-additive close of the base case (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}); it is \emph{not} on the
1200	  genus-$0$ critical path.
1201	\end{definition}

1203	\begin{lemma}
1204	[The $\mathbb G_m$-scaling action fixes the origin $0 \in \mathbb P^1$]
1205	  \label{lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero}
1206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero}
1207	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects, def:gaTranslationP1, def:p1bar_zero, def:gm_one}
1208	  % NOTE (iter-170 decision): the Lean body of `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero`
1209	  % remains a gated `sorry` — its discharge is strictly downstream of
1210	  % `gmScalingP1`'s body landing (per option (c) decomposition, this means
1211	  % landing in iter-173 at the earliest). Once `gmScalingP1` is concrete via
1212	  % the chart-glue, this collapse lemma reduces to a chart-1 direct computation
1213	  % via `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_morphismRestrict`.
1214	  % Archon-original; the fact is elementary (σ_×(0, λ) = λ · 0 = 0). No external % SOURCE QUOTE
1215	  % needed for the Lean encoding.
1216	  Let $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to \mathbb P^1$ be the scaling action
1217	  of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Then the composite
1218	  \[ \mathbb G_m \xrightarrow{\;(\mathrm{toUnit}_{\mathbb G_m} \fatsemi 0_{\mathbb P^1},\ \mathbf 1_{\mathbb G_m})\;}
1219	     \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_\times\;} \mathbb P^1 \]
1220	  equals the constant morphism $\mathrm{toUnit}_{\mathbb G_m} \fatsemi 0_{\mathbb P^1}$. That is,
1221	  $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ for every $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$.
1222	\end{lemma}

1224	\begin{proof}
1225	  \uses{def:gaTranslationP1, def:p1bar_zero, def:gm_one}
1226	  Chart-level. On the affine chart $\mathbb A^1 \times \mathbb G_m$ (coordinate $x$), the
1227	  morphism $\sigma_\times$ is the polynomial map $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda \cdot x$. Restricting
1228	  to the $\mathbb G_m$-section $x = 0$ gives the constant ring map
1229	  $\bar k[t, t^{-1}] \to \bar k$, $\lambda \mapsto 0$; equivalently the scheme morphism factors
1230	  through the closed point $0 \in \mathbb P^1$. The agreement with the other chart $u = 1/x$ near
1231	  $\infty$ is automatic (the section $x = 0$ does not meet that chart). The named equation is the
1232	  Lean encoding of this fact, with $\mathrm{toUnit}_{\mathbb G_m}$ supplying the
1233	  $\Spec \bar k$-factor and $\mathbf 1_{\mathbb G_m}$ the parameter $\lambda$.
1234	\end{proof}

1236	\begin{lemma}
1237	[$\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$: a homomorphism from the additive group into an abelian variety is trivial]
1238	  \label{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}
1240	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}

1240	  \uses{def:genus0_base_objects, def:gaTranslationP1, lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1241	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
1242	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
1243	  % NOTE (iter-164): the verbatim Milne Prop 3.9 statement+proof quotes are the prior-session-verified
1244	  % copies also carried on \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} below. This host lacks a PDF renderer
1245	  % (pdftoppm) so the quotes were NOT re-rendered from references/abelian-varieties.pdf this session;
1246	  % they are reproduced from the verified in-tree copy. See the writer report.
1247	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1248	
1249	  \textbf{Off the genus-$0$ critical path (iter-164).} This $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$
1250	  lemma is \emph{superseded by the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}
1251	  (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}), which closes the
1252	  base case without it. It is retained only as the classical Milne Proposition~3.9 ($\mathbb
1253	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility) argument feeding the alternative route
1254	  \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}; no block on the genus-$0$ critical path depends on it.
1255	
1256	  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$, and let
1257	  $\varphi \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ be a homomorphism of group objects (so $\varphi(0) = 0_A$ and
1258	  $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$). Then $\varphi$ is the trivial homomorphism: it
1259	  factors through the terminal object as the constant morphism at the identity,
1260	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1261	
1262	  \medskip
1263	  \noindent\textbf{Equivalent completeness phrasing.} Equivalently: a homomorphism from an
1264	  \emph{affine connected} group object into a \emph{complete} group object is trivial. That is the
1265	  abstract content; the proof below realises it characteristic-free.
1266	\end{lemma}
1267	
1268	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
1269	% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
1270	% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
1271	% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
1272	% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
1273	\begin{proof}
1274	  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product, lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
1275	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1276	  Write $A$ additively (an abelian variety is commutative). We give the conceptual
1277	  \emph{completeness} proof as the abstract argument, then record Milne's elementary $\mathbb
1278	  G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility, which applies whenever $\varphi$ extends to a morphism on
1279	  $\mathbb P^1$ (as it does in the genus-$0$ application).
1280	
1281	  \textbf{Completeness proof.} The image $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is a connected subgroup of $A$
1282	  (image of the connected $\mathbb G_a$ under a homomorphism). As the image of a homomorphism of
1283	  algebraic groups it is a \emph{closed} subgroup of $A$, hence \emph{complete} (closed in the
1284	  complete $A$); as a homomorphic image of the \emph{affine} $\mathbb G_a$ it is moreover
1285	  \emph{affine}. A connected variety that is simultaneously complete and affine over $\bar k$ is a
1286	  single point: its ring of global regular functions is a field finite over $\bar k$, hence equal
1287	  to $\bar k$ (the $\Gamma = \bar k$ collapse already packaged for proper integral sources in
1288	  \cref{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}). Therefore $\varphi(\mathbb G_a)$ is one point;
1289	  being a homomorphism, $\varphi$ sends $0$ there, so the point is $0_A$ and
1290	  $\varphi = \mathtt{toUnit}\,\mathbb G_a \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1291	
1292	  \medskip
1293	  \noindent\textbf{Milne's elementary $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility (when $\varphi$
1294	  extends to $\mathbb P^1$).} Suppose $\varphi = f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ for a morphism
1295	  $f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ (the genus-$0$ situation). Set $g := f - f(1)$, so $g(1) = 0_A$, and
1296	  form $h_\times := \sigma_\times \fatsemi g \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A$ with the
1297	  scaling companion $\sigma_\times$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}. Applying the additive
1298	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (complete),
1299	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $1 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so
1300	  $h_\times(1, 1) = g(1) = 0_A$), the axis restrictions are $x \mapsto g(x)$ and
1301	  $\lambda \mapsto g(\lambda)$, whence $g(\lambda x) = g(x) + g(\lambda)$ on $\bar k^\times$;
1302	  re-expanding $g$ gives the multiplicative relation
1303	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$ with $c = -\varphi(1)$. Now $\varphi$ is at once
1304	  additive, $\varphi(x + y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y)$, and ``multiplicative-additive'',
1305	  $\varphi(xy) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(y) + c$. Computing $\varphi((x + x')\,y)$ in two ways for
1306	  $x, x' \in \bar k$, $y \in \bar k^\times$,
1307	  \[ \varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(xy) + \varphi(x'y) = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') + 2\varphi(y) + 2c, \]
1308	  while also $\varphi((x+x')y) = \varphi(x+x') + \varphi(y) + c = \varphi(x) + \varphi(x') +
1309	  \varphi(y) + c$; equating gives $\varphi(y) = -c = \varphi(1)$ for every $y \in \bar k^\times$.
1310	  Hence $\varphi$ is constant on the dense $\mathbb G_m \subset \mathbb G_a$, so constant on
1311	  $\mathbb G_a$ (density, $A$ separated), with value $\varphi(0) = 0_A$. This is Milne's argument
1312	  verbatim and uses only the proven \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} plus the scaling action,
1313	  avoiding the ``image is a closed subgroup'' input of the completeness proof. \qedhere
1314	\end{proof}
1315	
1316	\begin{remark}
1317	  \label{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}
1318	  \textbf{The primary route is the $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut; why no Theorem~3.2 and no
1319	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ are needed.} The genus-$0$ base case ``$f \colon \mathbb P^1
1320	  \to A$ is constant'' is closed \emph{directly} by the proven additive decomposition
1321	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} composed with a total $\mathbb G$-action on $\mathbb P^1$,
1322	  with \emph{no} appeal to Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension), the theorem of
1323	  the cube, Auslander--Buchsbaum, or differentials. The reason the earlier draft thought
1324	  Theorem~3.2 was needed --- that the additive defect $\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)$ lives only
1325	  on the affine $\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a$ and must be extended over $\mathbb P^1 \times
1326	  \mathbb P^1$ --- evaporates once one composes with a \emph{total} action morphism of
1327	  \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}: the composite is already a total morphism out of $\mathbb P^1 \times
1328	  \mathbb G$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$, exactly what
1329	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (whose Lean signature needs only the first factor
1330	  complete) consumes.
1331	
1332	  \medskip
1333	  \noindent\textbf{Primary route --- the scaling shortcut (strategy decision of iter-164).} The
1334	  \emph{multiplicative} scaling action $\sigma_\times \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to
1335	  \mathbb P^1$, $(x, \lambda) \mapsto \lambda x$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}), closes the base case
1336	  in one shot, \emph{without} the additive homomorphism step or \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial} at
1337	  all. Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ (translate; here $0$ is a scaling fixed point). Form $h_\times :=
1338	  \sigma_\times \fatsemi f$ and apply \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} with $V = \mathbb P^1$,
1339	  $W = \mathbb G_m$, base points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$ and $1 \in \mathbb G_m$ (so $h_\times(0, 1) =
1340	  f(0) = 0_A$). Because $0$ is a \emph{fixed point} of scaling, the $W$-axis restriction collapses:

1347	  P^1$ (the dominant-source/separated-target handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}, i.e.\ the
1348	  $\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}$ family already used by \texttt{rigidity\_core}). This uses

1399	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
1400	  Normalise $f(0) = 0_A$ by a translation in $A$ (un-translate at the end). The proof is the
1401	  \emph{4th route} (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}): the additive defect map is never built on
1402	  the affine surface and never extended; instead we precompose $f$ with the \emph{total}
1403	  translation action $\sigma$, and the complete factor is $\mathbb P^1$ itself, so the proven
1404	  additive decomposition applies directly. Milne's Theorem~3.2
1405	  (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is \emph{not} used.
1406	
1407	  \textbf{$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is an additive homomorphism, via the total action $\sigma$.} Form
1408	  \[ h := \sigma \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to A, \qquad
1409	     \text{``}h(x, y) = f(x + y)\text{''}, \]
1410	  using the translation action $\sigma$ of \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}; this is a \emph{total}
1411	  morphism (no rational-map extension needed). Apply the additive decomposition
1412	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (Milne Cor~1.5) --- whose Lean signature requires only the
1413	  \emph{first} factor complete --- with $V = \mathbb P^1$ (proper), $W = \mathbb G_a$, and base
1414	  points $0 \in \mathbb P^1$, $0 \in \mathbb G_a$, noting $h(0, 0) = f(0) = 0_A$. The axis
1415	  restrictions are $x \mapsto h(x, 0) = f(x)$ and $y \mapsto h(0, y) = f(y)$, so the decomposition
1416	  $h = (p \fatsemi f) + (q \fatsemi (f|_{\mathbb G_a}))$ reads, on $\bar k$-points,

1419	  homomorphism of group objects (it sends $0 \mapsto 0_A$ and respects addition).

1422	  (the $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ lemma, the content of Milne's $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$
1423	  incompatibility), $f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ is the constant morphism at $0_A$. Hence $f$ is constant on
1424	  the dense open $\mathbb G_a \subset \mathbb P^1$; since $\mathbb G_a$ is dense and $A$ is
1425	  separated, $f$ is constant on all of $\mathbb P^1$, with value $f(0) = 0_A$ (i.e.\ the original
1426	  base value before the normalising translation). \qedhere

1435	  G_a$-additivity step and \cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}, and avoiding the ``image is a closed
1436	  subgroup'' Mathlib gap. The present $\mathbb G_a$-additive lemma is retained only for fidelity to
1437	  Milne; it is not on the genus-$0$ critical path.

1442	\begin{proposition}

1450	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
1451	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
1452	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
1453	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
1454	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
1455	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an

1459	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a

1471	  \leanok

1473	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We give the project's \textbf{primary} proof, the
1474	  \emph{$\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut}: it proves constancy \emph{in one shot} from the proven
1475	  additive decomposition (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and a scaling fixed point,
1476	  without the intermediate ``$f|_{\mathbb G_a}$ additive'' step and \emph{without} the
1477	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0$ triviality lemma. The theorem of the cube is \emph{not} used
1478	  (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}), and the map $f$ is already a morphism out of the complete
1479	  nonsingular $\mathbb P^1$, so Milne's Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) is
1480	  \emph{not} invoked either.

1482	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Choose the scaling fixed point $0 \in \mathbb G_a \subset
1483	  \mathbb P^1$ (one of the two fixed points of $\sigma_\times$). Translating in the group $A$ by
1484	  $-f(0)$, we may assume $f(0) = 0_A$; un-translating at the end restores the original value.

1487	  \[ h := \sigma_\times \fatsemi f \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_m \to A, \qquad

1494	  required. The decomposition writes $h = (p \fatsemi f_0) + (q \fatsemi g)$ with $f_0$ the
1495	  $V$-axis restriction $x \mapsto h(x, 1) = f(1 \cdot x) = f(x)$ (so $f_0 = f$) and $g$ the
1496	  $W$-axis restriction $\lambda \mapsto h(0, \lambda) = f(\lambda \cdot 0) = f(0) = 0_A$.
1497	
1498	  \textbf{The $W$-axis collapses (the load-bearing scaling fixed point).} Because $0$ is a
1499	  \emph{fixed point} of scaling, $\sigma_\times(0, \lambda) = 0$ for all $\lambda$
1500	  (\cref{lem:gmScaling_fixes_zero}), so the $W$-axis
1501	  restriction $g$ is the \emph{constant} morphism at $0_A$ --- the identity of the hom-group
1502	  $\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_m, A)$. The corresponding summand $q \fatsemi g$ is therefore the group
1503	  identity, and the decomposition collapses to
1504	  \[ h = p \fatsemi f, \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
1505	     \sigma_\times \fatsemi f = \mathrm{pr}_1 \fatsemi f, \qquad
1506	     \text{``}f(\lambda x) = f(x)\text{''} \quad (x \in \mathbb P^1,\ \lambda \in \mathbb G_m). \]
1507

1509	  \lambda)$): $f(\lambda) = f(1)$ for all $\lambda \in \mathbb G_m$, i.e.\ $f|_{\mathbb G_m}$ is
1510	  the constant morphism at $f(1)$. Since $\mathbb G_m$ is dense in the irreducible $\mathbb P^1$
1511	  and $A$ is separated, the two morphisms $f$ and $\mathrm{const}_{f(1)}$, agreeing on the
1512	  non-empty open $\mathbb G_m$, agree everywhere by the dominant-source/separated-target rigidity
1513	  handle \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (the same \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant} family
1514	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} uses). Hence $f$ is constant, with value $f(1)$; in the normalised
1515	  picture $f(0) = 0_A$, so the constant is $0_A$ (the original $f(0)$ before translation).
1516	
1517	  \medskip
1518	  \noindent\emph{Multi-factor remark.} For the general unirational statement of Proposition~3.10,
1519	  one writes $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition

1570	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
1571	
1572	\begin{proposition}
1573	
1574	\leanok
1575	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
1576	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1578	  \uses{def:genus}
1579	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
1580	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
1581	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
1582	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
1583	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
1584	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
1585	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
1586	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
1587	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
1588	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
1589	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
1590	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1591	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1592	\end{proposition}
1593	
1594	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
1595	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
1596	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
1597	\begin{proof}
1598	  \uses{def:genus}
1599	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
1600	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
1601	
1602	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
1603	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
1604	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
1605	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
1606	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
1607	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
1608	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
1609	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
1610	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
1611	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
1612	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
1613	\end{proof}
1614	
1615	\begin{remark}
1616	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
1617	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
1618	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
1619	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
1620	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
1621	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
1622	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
1623	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
1624	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
1625	\end{remark}
1626	
1627	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
1628	
1629	\begin{theorem}
1630	
1631	\leanok
1632	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
1633	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1634	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
1635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
1636	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1637	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
1638	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
1639	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
1640	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
1641	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
1642	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
1643	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1644	
1645	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
1646	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
1647	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
1648	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
1649	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
1650	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
1651	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
1652	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
1653	  \eta[A]$.
1654	\end{theorem}
1655	
1656	\begin{proof}
1657	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
1658	  \leanok
1659	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
1660	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
1661	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
1662	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
1663	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
1664	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
1665	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
1666	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
1667	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
1668	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
1669	
1670	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
1671	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
1672	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
1673	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
1674	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.

1	\chapter{The Jacobian as an abelian variety}
2	\label{chap:Jacobian}
3	
4	Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $k$ in the sense of Chapter~\ref{chap:Genus}, with genus $g = g(C)$.
5	
6	The Jacobian is built as the Albanese object of $C$ in the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, and the construction stratifies by genus. For positive genus the witness \emph{object} is the abelian variety $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ produced by \textbf{Route~A (the Picard scheme via FGA representability)}: this is the project's committed critical path, and it is \emph{mandatory} for the positive-genus object regardless of any other route choice --- the object must be a genuine $g$-dimensional abelian variety even when $C(k) = \emptyset$ (see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route~A and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$ and its Hilbert/Quot prerequisites are the single largest Mathlib build-out the project gates on. For genus $0$ the witness object is instead the trivial scheme $\Spec k$, \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A's representability engine; the only remaining content in that arm is a rigidity \emph{statement}, the characteristic-free keystone \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} via the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (Rigidity Lemma $+$ additivity Corollary~1.5 $+$ Corollary~1.2 $+$ rational-map extension), \emph{cube-free} and \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality argument. The over-$\bar k$ declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which still carries $[\mathtt{CharZero}]$, is the retained fallback-(a) artifact and is \emph{not} the declaration the genus-$0$ witness consumes.
7	
8	\section{The Albanese construction}
9	
10	For a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$, the Albanese variety is the universal abelian variety receiving a morphism from $C$. Over an algebraically closed field this is classically identified with the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme, but we construct it directly from symmetric powers.
11	
12	\subsection{Symmetric powers and the Abel--Jacobi map}
13	
14	Let $C^{(g)} = \Sym^g(C)$ be the $g$\textsuperscript{th} symmetric power of $C$ (the quotient of $C^g$ by the action of the symmetric group $S_g$). It is a smooth proper scheme over $k$ of dimension $g$. Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
15	\[
16	  \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
17	  \sum_{i=1}^g [P_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g P_i - g P_0\bigr)
18	\]
19	is surjective and birational onto its image; its fibres are projective spaces (Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch). Choosing $P_0$ gives a translation isomorphism $\Pic^g_{C/k} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Pic^0_{C/k}$, and the Stein factorisation of $\sigma$ yields the Jacobian as the connected component of the identity.
20	
21	In the Lean formalisation we do not have general symmetric powers of schemes (or quotients by finite group actions) in Mathlib. We therefore take a more direct approach: we define the Jacobian as the Albanese object of $C$ inside the category of smooth proper group schemes over $k$, using the universal property and the known dimension formula.
22	
23	\subsection{The Albanese universal property}
24	
25	\begin{definition}\leanok
26	[Albanese universal property of a pointed curve]
27	  \label{def:IsAlbanese}
28	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}
29	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Alb (the Albanese map), FGA Explained ch.~5; arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
30	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Reversing the preceding argument, we see that every
31	  % such $b$ arises from a unique map $a\:B^*\to \IPic_{X/k}$ such that
32	  % $a(0)=0$.  Since $B^*$ is integral, $a$ factors through $P$.  Thus the
33	  % maps $a\:B^*\to P$ and $b\:X\to B$ are in bijective correspondence.
34	  % Plainly, this correspondence is compatible with maps $b'\:B\to B'$ such
35	  % that $b'(0)=0$.  In particular, $1_P$ corresponds to a natural map
36	  % $u\:X\to A$ such that $u(x)=0$, and every map $b\:X\to B$ factors
37	  % uniquely through $u$."
38	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Albanese map, rmk:Alb).}
39	  Let $(C, P)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with a marked $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$. A smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ is an \emph{Albanese object} for $(C, P)$ if there exists a morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ sending $P$ to the identity element $\eta \in J$, and such that every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ factors uniquely as $f = \iota \circ g$ for some morphism of group schemes $g \colon J \to A$.
40	\end{definition}
41	
42	\begin{remark}
43	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_typeclasses}
44	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
45	  The Lean encoding of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese} takes the four abelian-variety conditions on $J$ --- group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility --- as \emph{typeclass parameters} of the declaration \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese}, not as conjuncts inside the body. The body is the existential statement on $\iota$ and its universal-factorisation property; the four hypotheses on $J$ sit on the binder of the definition. A downstream user constructs an \texttt{IsAlbanese} term against these instances and threads them at every use site (cf.\ the \texttt{letI} chain in \texttt{AbelJacobi.ofCurve} which installs all four instances on $\Jac(C)$ before applying any Albanese consequence). The same convention is used for the target abelian variety $A$ in the universal-property clause.
46	\end{remark}
47	
48	\subsection{Extracting the universal morphism}
49	
50	An \texttt{IsAlbanese} term $h$ packages, behind a single existential, the universal pointed morphism $\iota \colon C \to J$ together with its two characterising properties: the pointed condition $P \circ \iota = \eta_J$ and the universal-factorisation property of $\iota$. The data and the two properties are extracted from the existential body by the following trio of helpers, each implemented in Lean via \texttt{Classical.choose} / \texttt{Classical.choose\_spec} on the existential content of \texttt{IsAlbanese}.
51	
52	\begin{definition}
53	
54	\leanok
55	[The Albanese universal morphism]
56	  \label{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
57	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.ofCurve}
58	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese}
59	  Given a pointed curve $(C, P)$ and an Albanese term $h \colon \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$, the \emph{universal morphism} of $h$ is the morphism
60	  \[
61	    \iota := h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J,
62	  \]
63	  obtained by \texttt{Classical.choose} on the existential body of $h$.
64	\end{definition}
65	
66	\begin{lemma}
67	
68	\leanok
69	[Pointed condition for the universal morphism]
70	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}
71	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.comp_ofCurve}
72	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
73	  The universal morphism $h.\mathtt{ofCurve}$ sends the marked point $P$ to the identity of $J$:
74	  \[
75	    P \circ h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \;=\; \eta_J.
76	  \]
77	\end{lemma}
78	
79	\begin{lemma}
80	
81	\leanok
82	[Universal factorisation through the Albanese object]
83	  \label{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
84	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}
85	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
86	  For every morphism $f \colon C \to A$ from $C$ to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, there exists a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = h.\mathtt{ofCurve} \circ g$.
87	\end{lemma}
88	
89	This extraction API is precisely what the protected interface $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} consumes: each protected wrapper takes a marked point $P \in C(k)$ as input, projects the per-point Albanese data from the bundled witness \texttt{JacobianWitness} (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) at that $P$, and applies the corresponding helper above.
90	
91	\begin{theorem}\leanok
92	[Uniqueness of the Albanese object]
93	  \label{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
94	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAlbanese.unique}
95	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}
96	  Let $J_1, J_2$ be two Albanese objects for the same pointed curve $(C, P)$, with universal pointed morphisms $\iota_1 := h_1.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_1$ and $\iota_2 := h_2.\mathtt{ofCurve} \colon C \to J_2$ (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}). Then there is a unique morphism $e \colon J_1 \to J_2$ satisfying $\iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e$. The proof is the standard universal-property argument: each of the two universal morphisms factors through the other, the two composites equal the unique self-factorisation $\mathrm{id}$, and the produced morphism $e$ is therefore an isomorphism; only the existence and uniqueness of $e$ as a morphism are exposed in the conclusion (see Remark~\ref{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}).
97	\end{theorem}
98	
99	\begin{remark}
100	  \label{rem:IsAlbanese_unique_iso}
101	  \uses{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
102	  The Lean proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique} internally produces an inverse morphism $h \colon J_2 \to J_1$ and verifies $g \circ h = \mathrm{id}_{J_1}$ and $h \circ g = \mathrm{id}_{J_2}$ (the invertibility witnesses are computed as intermediate lemmas of the tactic block) before returning the triple $(g,\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ g,\ \text{uniqueness})$. The invertibility witnesses are computed but not retained in the return type
103	  \[
104	    \exists! (e : J_1 \to J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.
105	  \]
106	  The natural strengthening would be to return
107	  \[
108	    \exists! (e : J_1 \cong J_2),\ \iota_2 = \iota_1 \circ e.\mathtt{hom},
109	  \]
110	  packaging the produced morphism together with its inverse. Tightening the conclusion in this way is a Lean-side refactor of the signature of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} that the project may carry out in a future iteration. The downstream consumers of \texttt{IsAlbanese.unique} (the protected \texttt{AbelJacobi.Jacobian.*} interface) use only the morphism-and-uniqueness content of the conclusion, so the current weaker conclusion is sufficient for the present API.
111	\end{remark}
112	
113	\subsection{The Jacobian definition}
114	
115	\begin{definition}\leanok
116	[Jacobian]
117	  \label{def:Jacobian}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian}
119	  \uses{def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
120	  The \emph{Jacobian} of $C$ is the abelian variety $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ obtained as the Albanese object of $C$: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $k$ of dimension $g(C)$ equipped with a universal morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ (for each choice of marked point). It is viewed as an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$. Formally, $\Jac(C)$ is defined as the underlying scheme of a (uniform-over-$P$) Albanese witness for $C$ provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}.
121	
122	  When $g(C) = 0$ the Jacobian collapses to $\Spec k$ itself, which is the terminal object in the category of $k$-schemes. When $g(C) > 0$ the underlying scheme is the abelian variety produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}; the genus-one case recovers $C$ itself (after choosing a $k$-rational point), and the higher genus case yields the non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety. Both cases are handled uniformly: $\Jac(C)$ is the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness, with no special-case branching at the level of the definition. The genus-$0$ specialisation is implicit in the fact that any group scheme over $k$ of relative dimension $0$ is isomorphic to $\Spec k$.
123	\end{definition}
124	
125	\section{Group scheme structure and abelian-variety properties}
126	
127	The four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ are each obtained, at the level of the Lean formalisation, by projecting a field of the underlying Albanese witness produced by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. The mathematical content of each property is the corresponding statement about the Albanese variety of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve.
128	
129	\begin{theorem}\leanok
130	[Group object structure]
131	  \label{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGrpObj}
133	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
134	  $\Jac(C)$ carries a canonical group-object structure in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	\begin{proof}
138	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, thm:IsAlbanese_unique}
139	  By Definition~\ref{def:Jacobian}, $\Jac(C) = (\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C).J$ as a $k$-scheme, where $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$ is the Albanese witness extracted from Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} via \texttt{Classical.choice}. The witness carries the group-object structure as its field $\mathtt{grpObj} : \mathrm{GrpObj}\, J$, so $\Jac(C)$ inherits it by projection.
140	
141	  Mathematically, any Albanese variety carries a unique group-object structure compatible with its universal pointed morphism. Indeed, if $\iota \colon C \to J$ is the universal morphism sending the marked point $P$ to the identity, then the diagonal $C \times C \to J \times J$, post-composed with the universal morphism out of $C \times C$ (factored through $J$ by Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}), produces the multiplication; the unique morphism to a one-point pointed scheme produces the inverse; the marked point produces the identity. Uniqueness of the resulting group-object structure is again the universal-property argument of Theorem~\ref{thm:IsAlbanese_unique}.
142	\end{proof}
143	
144	\begin{theorem}\leanok
145	[Smoothness and dimension]
146	  \label{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus}
148	  \uses{def:Jacobian, def:genus}
149	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Cor.~cor:sm (smoothness/dimension over a field) + Cor.~cor:ch0 (char-$0$ smoothness) + Prp.~prp:P0 (smooth over reduced $S$); arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~34, 38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
150	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:sm): "Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.
151	  % Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\et}$.  Then
152	  % $$\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).$$ Equality holds if and
153	  % only if $\IPic_{X/k}$ is smooth at $0;$ if so, then $\IPic_{X/k}$ is
154	  % smooth of dimension $\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})$ everywhere."
155	  % SOURCE QUOTE (cor:ch0): "Assume $S$ is the spectrum of a field $k$.
156	  % Assume $\IPic_{X/k}$ exists and represents $\Pic_{(X/k)\et}$. If $k$ is
157	  % of characteristic $0$, then $\IPic_{X/k}$ is smooth of dimension
158	  % $\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})$ everywhere."
159	  % SOURCE QUOTE (prp:P0, smoothness clause): "Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists,
160	  % and represents $\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.  For $s\in S$, assume all the
161	  % $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ are smooth of the same dimension.  Then $\IPic_{X/S}$
162	  % has an open group subscheme $\IPicz_{X/S}$ of finite type whose fibers
163	  % are the $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$.  Furthermore, if $S$ is reduced, then
164	  % $\IPicz_{X/S}$ is smooth over $S$."
165	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Cor.~(cor:sm), Cor.~(cor:ch0), Prp.~(prp:P0).}
166	  $\Jac(C) \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$.
167	\end{theorem}
168	
169	\begin{proof}
170	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness, def:genus}
171	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{smoothGenus} : \mathrm{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,(\genus\, C)\,J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness $\mathrm{jacobianWitness}\, C$; the relative dimension $g(C)$ is recorded directly in the witness so that no separate dimension calculation is needed on the Lean side.
172	
173	  Mathematically, the dimension of an Albanese variety of $C$ equals the genus $g = g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ (Phase~A, Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafAb}). The tangent space to $\Jac(C)$ at the identity is canonically identified with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ by deformation theory of the Picard functor: an infinitesimal deformation of $\mathcal O_C$ is classified by an element of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$, and this identifies the tangent space at the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$. Smoothness of $\Jac(C)$ over $k$ follows from the smoothness of any group scheme over a field whose tangent space at the identity is of constant rank equal to the relative dimension --- a consequence of homogeneity of the group action, since translation by any $k$-rational point is an automorphism and so the local structure of $\Jac(C)$ at every $k$-rational point is the same as at the identity.
174	\end{proof}
175	
176	\begin{theorem}\leanok
177	[Properness]
178	  \label{thm:Jacobian_proper}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instIsProper}
180	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
181	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Prp.~prp:P0 ($\IPicz_{X/S}$ closed in $\IPic_{X/S}$ and proper over $S$ when fibres are complete); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~38  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
182	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume $\IPic_{X/S}$ exists, and represents
183	  % $\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$.  For $s\in S$, assume all the $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ are
184	  % smooth of the same dimension.  Then $\IPic_{X/S}$ has an open group
185	  % subscheme $\IPicz_{X/S}$ of finite type whose fibers are the
186	  % $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$.  Furthermore, if $S$ is reduced, then $\IPicz_{X/S}$
187	  % is smooth over $S$.  Moreover, if all the $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ are complete
188	  % and if $\IPic_{X/S}$ is separated over $S$, then $\IPicz_{X/S}$ is closed
189	  % in $\IPic_{X/S}$ and proper over $S$."
190	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Prp.~(prp:P0).}
191	  $\Jac(C) \to \Spec k$ is proper.
192	\end{theorem}
193	
194	\begin{proof}
195	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
196	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{proper} : \mathrm{IsProper}\, J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness.
197	
198	  Mathematically, properness of the Albanese variety is part of its construction. In the Picard-scheme route, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is a closed subgroup scheme of the relative Picard scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, which is itself proper over $\Spec k$ because $C$ is proper and geometrically connected (FGA representability for $\Pic$ of a smooth proper geometrically connected curve produces a proper group scheme; cf.\ FGA Explained, Chapter~9). In the symmetric-power route, properness of $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ (and hence $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ after translation) follows from properness of $\Sym^g(C)$ together with the universal closedness of the surjection $\sigma$. In either case, properness descends to $\Jac(C)$.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{theorem}\leanok
202	[Geometric irreducibility]
203	  \label{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.instGeometricallyIrreducible}
205	  \uses{def:Jacobian}
